title: Pecan and bourbon tart
subtitle: An American-style pecan filling in a shortcrust case, lifted with bourbon
excerpt: A deep-filled tart of whole pecans suspended in a glossy bourbon-and-brown-sugar custard, baked until set. Rich, not too sweet, and very good with cream.
sourceNotes: Pecan pie is documented in American cooking from the late nineteenth century, with the earliest printed recipes appearing in newspapers from the Gulf Coast states in the 1880s. The inclusion of corn syrup in American recipes dates from the 1920s when Karo began promoting their product. This version uses golden syrup, consistent with British interpretations of the same dish. The bourbon addition follows the Kentucky tradition documented in twentieth-century American regional cookbooks.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind baked a day ahead. The filling can be made and refrigerated for up to 24 hours. Fill and bake on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 200°C. Reduce to 170°C fan once filling is added.

glossaryTerms.definition: Baking a pastry case without its filling, weighed down with baking paper and beans or weights, to set the base and prevent it from becoming soggy when the filling is added.

content.text: Pecan pie has been made across the American South since the late nineteenth century. The filling is a custard of eggs, sugar, and golden syrup with whole pecans stirred through; it sets to a dense, fudgy consistency as it bakes. Bourbon is not universal in American recipes but has been part of the Kentucky tradition for generations, cutting the sweetness and adding a complexity the filling needs.

before the filling goes in; this prevents a soggy base under the dense, wet filling. The tart needs to cool for at least two hours after baking — the filling continues to set as it comes to room temperature.

attrs.prepNote: cold, cut into cubes, for the pastry

content.text: Rub the pastry-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add the pastry {{egg-yolks}} and 2–3 tablespoons of cold water; mix to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 60 minutes.

Roll the pastry to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Prick the base, line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Blind bake for 15 minutes, then remove the paper and beans and bake for a further 8 minutes until the base is lightly golden. Leave to cool slightly.

Reduce the oven to 170°C fan. Whisk {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, the filling-quantity melted {{unsalted-butter}}, {{eggs}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together until smooth. Scatter {{pecans}} over the base of the tart case. Pour the filling over them slowly; the pecans will float and arrange themselves. Bake for 30–35 minutes until the filling is set at the edges and has a gentle wobble in the centre — it should read 85°C on a probe thermometer. Cool completely before slicing.
The filling sets further as it cools. A tart that wobbles in the centre at 30 minutes will be fully set after two hours at room temperature.
To add bourbon: stir 2 tablespoons into the filling with the vanilla. The alcohol bakes out; the flavour deepens the tart.
Toast the pecans in a dry pan for two minutes before adding them to the tart case; it intensifies their flavour noticeably.
